_schema: Accounting software for trades/contractors — Phase 2 category added 2026-05-28. Trade-agnostic; grouped by positioning. Pricing verified-as-of verified_date and changes often (esp. QuickBooks). Several have real affiliate programs (FreshBooks, Xero) but affiliate_url stays 'TBD' until tracking is confirmed.

software.name: QuickBooks Online
software.tagline: The default small-business accounting platform — and what your FSM syncs to
software.pricing_summary: Five plans: Solopreneur $20/mo, Simple Start $38/mo, Essentials $75/mo, Plus $115/mo (most popular), Advanced $275/mo. Frequent annual price increases (12–25%). New subscribers often get 50% off the first 3 months
software.long_description:
QuickBooks Online (QBO) is the default accounting platform for US small businesses, and for trades it's effectively the gravitational center of the software stack: nearly every field-service tool in this directory lists a QuickBooks integration first, because that's where contractors' books live. Made by Intuit (founded 1983, Mountain View), QBO does full double-entry accounting — invoicing, expense and bank-feed reconciliation, A/R and A/P, sales tax, and deep reporting — and supports an enormous ecosystem of accountants, bookkeepers, and apps.

For a contractor, the practical reality is that your bookkeeper or CPA almost certainly knows QuickBooks, and your FSM almost certainly syncs to it, which makes QBO the path of least resistance. Five tiers scale from Solopreneur ($20/mo) and Simple Start ($38) up through Essentials, Plus ($115, the most popular for trades that need inventory and job costing), and Advanced ($275).

The trade-offs are price and price increases. Intuit raises QBO pricing aggressively — 12–25% bumps in recent years, including the largest single increase in QBO history in 2026 — and the higher tiers get expensive. The interface can also feel cluttered, and some features push you toward Intuit's add-ons (Payments, Payroll). But given the universal FSM integration and accountant familiarity, QBO is the safe default for most trades businesses; the alternatives win on price (Wave), simplicity (FreshBooks), or unlimited users (Xero).
software.how_it_works: QuickBooks Online is cloud accounting: you connect your bank and credit-card feeds, and transactions flow in for categorization and reconciliation. You invoice customers, track expenses and mileage, manage A/R and A/P, handle sales tax, and run financial reports (P&L, balance sheet, job costing on higher tiers). Crucially for trades, your FSM (Jobber, Housecall Pro, ServiceTitan, etc.) syncs customers, invoices, and payments into QBO so you don't double-enter. Add-ons like QuickBooks Payments and Payroll plug in. You pick a tier by the features you need (inventory and job costing start at Plus), with a 30-day free trial and frequent 50%-off-3-months promos for new subscribers.

pros_detail.title: Universal FSM integration
pros_detail.explanation: Virtually every field-service platform integrates with QuickBooks first. Choosing QBO means your scheduling/invoicing tool syncs cleanly to your books with the least friction — a major practical advantage for trades.

pros_detail.title: Accountant and bookkeeper familiarity
pros_detail.explanation: QuickBooks is the standard most CPAs and bookkeepers know cold. Your accountant can work in it without a learning curve, and finding QuickBooks-fluent help is easy.

pros_detail.title: Full-featured, scales with you
pros_detail.explanation: From basic invoicing to inventory, job costing, and advanced reporting on higher tiers, QBO grows with the business — you won't outgrow it the way you might a lighter tool.

pros_detail.title: Huge app ecosystem
pros_detail.explanation: Thousands of apps and add-ons (payments, payroll, expense, time tracking) connect to QBO, so you can extend it for almost any trades workflow.

pros_detail.title: Deep reporting and tax readiness
pros_detail.explanation: Strong financial reporting, sales-tax handling, and tax-time exports make year-end and accountant handoff smoother than lighter tools manage.

cons_detail.title: Aggressive, frequent price increases
cons_detail.explanation: Intuit raises QBO prices 12–25% in recent years, including the largest single hike in its history in 2026. What's affordable today gets pricier, and there's little you can do but absorb it or switch.

cons_detail.title: Higher tiers get expensive
cons_detail.explanation: Inventory and job costing require Plus ($115/mo), and Advanced is $275/mo. For a small trades shop, the tier you actually need can cost meaningfully more than Xero or FreshBooks.

cons_detail.title: Cluttered, complex interface
cons_detail.explanation: QBO is powerful but can feel busy and complex for a non-accountant owner. Simpler tools (FreshBooks, Wave) are friendlier if you don't need QBO's depth.

cons_detail.title: Upsell pressure toward Intuit add-ons
cons_detail.explanation: QBO nudges you toward QuickBooks Payments and Payroll, which may not be the cheapest options. Watch for add-on costs that inflate the true monthly bill.

cons_detail.title: Per-feature gating
cons_detail.explanation: Key capabilities are locked to specific tiers, so you can be forced up a plan for one feature — model which tier you truly need rather than assuming the entry price.

software.best_for: Most trades businesses that want the safe default — universal FSM integration, accountant familiarity, and room to scale — and can absorb Intuit's pricing
software.weaknesses: Aggressive annual price increases (12–25%, largest-ever hike in 2026); higher tiers expensive (Plus $115, Advanced $275); interface can feel cluttered; upsell pressure toward Intuit Payments/Payroll

ratings.reddit_sentiment: the default everyone integrates with; widely used but frequently criticized for relentless price increases

software.affiliate_payout_note: Intuit runs a QuickBooks affiliate program, but it's limited/curated; affiliate_url stays TBD until tracking is confirmed

faqs.q: How much does QuickBooks Online cost in 2026?
faqs.a: Five plans: Solopreneur $20/mo, Simple Start $38/mo, Essentials $75/mo, Plus $115/mo (most popular for trades needing job costing/inventory), and Advanced $275/mo. There's a 30-day free trial and frequent 50%-off-3-months promos. Note Intuit raises prices aggressively (12–25% in recent years).

faqs.q: Why do most contractors use QuickBooks?
faqs.a: Two reasons: nearly every field-service tool integrates with QuickBooks first (so your FSM syncs to your books cleanly), and most CPAs and bookkeepers already know it. That combination of universal integration and accountant familiarity makes QBO the path of least resistance for trades, even when cheaper alternatives exist.

faqs.q: QuickBooks Online vs Xero for a trades business?
faqs.a: QuickBooks wins on FSM integration breadth and accountant familiarity in the US; Xero wins on price predictability and unlimited users (its plans aren't priced per user). If your accountant and FSM are QuickBooks-centric, QBO is easiest; if you want to add team members without rising cost and prefer cleaner pricing, Xero is worth a serious look.

faqs.q: Which QuickBooks Online plan do trades need?
faqs.a: Solo operators can start on Simple Start ($38). Most growing trades shops land on Plus ($115/mo) because inventory and job costing — which matter for materials and project profitability — start at that tier. Don't pay for Advanced ($275) unless you need its advanced reporting and user limits.

faqs.q: Is QuickBooks Online worth the price increases?
faqs.a: For most trades, yes — the universal FSM integration and accountant familiarity usually outweigh the cost, and switching accounting platforms is disruptive. But if price is the priority, Wave (free) or FreshBooks/Xero (cheaper at comparable tiers) are legitimate alternatives, especially for simpler bookkeeping needs.

software.name: Xero
software.tagline: Full-featured accounting with unlimited users and predictable pricing
software.pricing_summary: Three plans (US): Early $25/mo, Growing $55/mo, Established $90/mo. Pricing is not per-user — you can add team members without the cost rising. Free trial available
software.long_description:
Xero is the strongest full-accounting alternative to QuickBooks — a cloud platform with 4.2M+ subscribers globally that does complete double-entry accounting (bank reconciliation, invoicing, A/R and A/P, reporting) with a cleaner interface and, crucially, pricing that isn't charged per user. Founded in 2006 in Wellington, New Zealand, it earns slightly higher review scores than QBO (4.5/5 Capterra across 850+ reviews, 4.3/5 G2) and a 93% recommend rate.

The headline advantage for a growing trades business is the unlimited-users model: you can add office staff, field techs, and your accountant without your software bill climbing, which is a real divergence from per-seat tools. Its three US tiers — Early ($25), Growing ($55), and Established ($90) — are simpler and more predictable than QBO's five-tier ladder and aggressive hikes.

The trade-offs are US ecosystem depth and entry-tier limits. QuickBooks still has broader FSM integration and deeper accountant familiarity in the US market, so some trades tools integrate with QBO more natively than Xero. And Xero's entry 'Early' plan caps invoices/bills, so most real businesses need Growing or above. But for a contractor who wants full accounting, more users at no extra cost, and a cleaner experience than QuickBooks, Xero is the leading alternative — and it has an affiliate/partner program, unlike most options here.
software.how_it_works: Xero is cloud accounting: connect bank feeds, reconcile transactions, send invoices, track bills and expenses, manage A/R and A/P, and run reports — all in a notably clean interface. Unlike per-seat tools, you can add unlimited users (staff and your accountant) at no extra cost. You pick from three US tiers (Early/Growing/Established) based on transaction volume and features; the entry Early plan limits invoices and bills, so growing businesses use Growing or above. Xero connects to many field-service and payments apps, and your accountant can collaborate directly in the file. A free trial lets you test it before subscribing.

pros_detail.title: Unlimited users at no extra cost
pros_detail.explanation: Xero doesn't charge per user, so you can add office staff, techs, and your accountant without the bill rising — a meaningful saving versus per-seat tools as a trades business grows.

pros_detail.title: Cleaner, more modern interface
pros_detail.explanation: Reviewers consistently find Xero's interface cleaner and friendlier than QuickBooks', lowering the learning curve for owners who aren't accountants.

pros_detail.title: Slightly higher ratings + strong recommend rate
pros_detail.explanation: 4.5/5 Capterra (850+ reviews), 4.3/5 G2, and a 93% recommend rate edge out QBO — a signal of strong user satisfaction with the full-accounting experience.

pros_detail.title: Predictable three-tier pricing
pros_detail.explanation: Three simple tiers ($25/$55/$90) are easier to reason about than QBO's five-tier ladder, and Xero hasn't been associated with the same aggressive annual hikes.

pros_detail.title: Real affiliate/partner program
pros_detail.explanation: Xero runs an affiliate/partner program, which (unlike most categories here where programs are TBD) gives WrenchStack a genuine path to monetize recommending it — aligned with recommending a strong product.

cons_detail.title: Less native US FSM integration than QuickBooks
cons_detail.explanation: QuickBooks still has the broadest, most-native field-service integrations in the US. Some trades tools sync to QBO more deeply than to Xero, so confirm your specific FSM's Xero integration quality.

cons_detail.title: Entry plan is limited
cons_detail.explanation: The Early plan caps invoices and bills, so most real trades businesses need Growing ($55) or above — the true entry cost is higher than the $25 headline.

cons_detail.title: US accountant familiarity is lower
cons_detail.explanation: More US bookkeepers and CPAs default to QuickBooks. Your accountant may be less fluent in Xero, which can add friction at tax time or for outsourced bookkeeping.

cons_detail.title: Payroll/payments via add-ons
cons_detail.explanation: Like QBO, full payroll and some capabilities come via integrations/add-ons (e.g., Gusto for payroll), so the all-in stack cost is more than the base subscription.

cons_detail.title: Smaller US mindshare
cons_detail.explanation: Despite global scale, Xero has less US brand mindshare than QuickBooks, so there's more 'is this the safe choice?' hesitation among traditional trades owners and their advisors.

software.best_for: Growing trades businesses that want full accounting with unlimited users, predictable pricing, and a cleaner interface than QuickBooks — and whose FSM integrates well with Xero
software.weaknesses: Less native US FSM integration and lower accountant familiarity than QuickBooks; entry 'Early' plan caps invoices/bills (most need Growing+); payroll via add-ons

ratings.reddit_sentiment: well-liked as the cleaner, unlimited-users alternative to QuickBooks; main hesitation is US integration/accountant familiarity

software.affiliate_payout_note: Xero runs an affiliate/partner program; affiliate_url stays TBD until tracking is confirmed

faqs.q: How much does Xero cost?
faqs.a: Three US plans: Early $25/mo, Growing $55/mo, and Established $90/mo, with a free trial. Importantly, Xero isn't priced per user — you can add team members and your accountant without the cost rising. The entry Early plan caps invoices/bills, so most growing businesses use Growing or above.

faqs.q: Is Xero better than QuickBooks for contractors?
faqs.a: It depends on priorities. Xero offers unlimited users, cleaner pricing, a more modern interface, and slightly higher ratings; QuickBooks offers broader US FSM integration and deeper accountant familiarity. If your FSM integrates well with Xero and you value adding users without cost increases, Xero is excellent; if your stack and accountant are QuickBooks-centric, QBO is easier.

faqs.q: Does Xero charge per user?
faqs.a: No — Xero's pricing is not based on user count, so you can add employees, field staff, and your accountant without your subscription cost going up. That's a key advantage over per-seat tools for a growing trades business with multiple people needing access.

faqs.q: Will my field-service software sync with Xero?
faqs.a: Many do — Xero integrates with a wide range of apps — but QuickBooks still has the broadest, most-native US field-service integrations. Before switching, confirm that your specific FSM (Jobber, Housecall Pro, etc.) has a solid Xero integration, since QBO syncs are sometimes deeper.

faqs.q: Does Xero handle payroll?
faqs.a: Xero offers payroll capabilities and integrates with dedicated payroll providers (like Gusto) for full payroll in the US. As with QuickBooks, plan for payroll as part of the stack — often via an add-on or integration — rather than assuming it's fully included in the base accounting subscription.

software.name: FreshBooks
software.tagline: Invoicing-first accounting for solo and small service businesses
software.pricing_summary: Lite $21/mo (up to 5 billable clients), Plus $38/mo (up to 50 clients), Premium $65/mo, Enterprise custom. Annual billing lowers the rate. Additional team members are an extra per-user fee
software.long_description:
FreshBooks is invoicing-first accounting built for solo operators and small service businesses — the contractor or freelancer who cares more about sending professional invoices and getting paid than running deep double-entry books. Founded in 2003 in Toronto, it's earned a strong reputation for ease of use, with matching 4.5/5 ratings on both G2 (790+ reviews) and Capterra (4,000+ reviews), where reviewers single out the invoicing workflow and fast onboarding.

For a small trades operator, the appeal is simplicity: polished invoices, time tracking, expense capture, proposals, and online payments in a tool that doesn't require accounting knowledge to use. Pricing runs Lite ($21/mo, up to 5 billable clients), Plus ($38, up to 50 clients), and Premium ($65), with annual billing discounts. It also runs a genuine affiliate program, which is relatively rare in this set.

The trade-offs come from its invoicing-first design. The billable-client limits on lower tiers can pinch a growing shop, additional team members cost extra per user, and its accounting depth (inventory, advanced reporting, double-entry rigor) is lighter than QuickBooks or Xero — historically FreshBooks was cash-based and single-entry, though it's added double-entry features. For a solo or small service business that prioritizes invoicing and getting paid over full accounting, FreshBooks is a delight; shops that need deep accounting, inventory, or many users will outgrow it toward QuickBooks or Xero.
software.how_it_works: FreshBooks centers on the bill-and-get-paid workflow: you create branded invoices (one-off or recurring), track time and expenses against clients/projects, send proposals and estimates, and accept online card/ACH payments that reconcile against invoices. Bank feeds and reporting cover the bookkeeping basics, and it's added double-entry accounting features over time. You choose a tier by your number of billable clients (Lite up to 5, Plus up to 50) — a model that fits service businesses with a defined client base — and add team members for an extra per-user fee. A 30-day free trial lets you test the invoicing flow before subscribing.

pros_detail.title: Best-in-class invoicing experience
pros_detail.explanation: FreshBooks is built around invoicing — professional, branded invoices, recurring billing, and easy online payment — which is exactly the workflow a solo trades operator needs most. Reviewers consistently praise it.

pros_detail.title: Genuinely easy to use
pros_detail.explanation: Matching 4.5/5 ratings on G2 and Capterra and fast onboarding reflect how approachable it is for non-accountants. A contractor can run it without accounting training.

pros_detail.title: Time tracking + proposals built in
pros_detail.explanation: Time tracking, expenses, and proposals/estimates are integrated, so a small service business can quote, track, and bill in one tool tied to clients and projects.

pros_detail.title: Affordable entry + annual discounts
pros_detail.explanation: Lite at $21/mo (less with annual billing) is an accessible entry point for solo operators, cheaper than QuickBooks' comparable functionality for simple needs.

pros_detail.title: Real affiliate program
pros_detail.explanation: FreshBooks runs an established affiliate program — relatively rare in this set — giving WrenchStack a genuine monetization path on a product it can recommend on merit for small service businesses.

cons_detail.title: Billable-client limits on lower tiers
cons_detail.explanation: Lite caps at 5 billable clients and Plus at 50. A growing trades shop with many customers can hit these limits and be pushed to a higher tier sooner than expected.

cons_detail.title: Per-user fees for the team
cons_detail.explanation: Additional team members cost extra per user, so a multi-person shop's true cost climbs beyond the headline plan price — unlike Xero's unlimited-users model.

cons_detail.title: Lighter accounting depth
cons_detail.explanation: FreshBooks is invoicing-first; its inventory, advanced reporting, and accounting rigor are lighter than QuickBooks or Xero. Shops that need deep accounting or job costing will outgrow it.

cons_detail.title: Historically single-entry roots
cons_detail.explanation: FreshBooks started cash-based/single-entry and added double-entry features later. Accountants who want full double-entry rigor from day one sometimes still prefer QuickBooks or Xero.

cons_detail.title: Fewer native FSM integrations
cons_detail.explanation: Trades field-service tools integrate with QuickBooks (and increasingly Xero) more than FreshBooks, so a contractor relying on FSM-to-accounting sync should confirm FreshBooks support.

software.best_for: Solo and small service contractors (handymen, cleaners, painters, small crews) who prioritize easy professional invoicing and getting paid over deep double-entry accounting
software.weaknesses: Billable-client limits on lower tiers; extra per-user fees for team members; lighter accounting depth than QuickBooks/Xero; fewer native FSM integrations

ratings.reddit_sentiment: loved by solo and small service businesses for invoicing ease; seen as light on deep accounting for larger shops

software.affiliate_payout_note: FreshBooks runs an established affiliate program; affiliate_url stays TBD until tracking is confirmed

faqs.q: How much does FreshBooks cost?
faqs.a: Lite is $21/mo (up to 5 billable clients), Plus $38/mo (up to 50 clients), Premium $65/mo, and Enterprise is custom. Annual billing lowers the effective rate. Note that additional team members cost an extra per-user fee, so a multi-person shop's real cost is higher than the base plan.

faqs.q: Is FreshBooks real accounting or just invoicing?
faqs.a: It's invoicing-first accounting. FreshBooks excels at invoicing, time tracking, expenses, and getting paid, and it has added double-entry accounting features over time. But its accounting depth (inventory, advanced reporting) is lighter than QuickBooks or Xero — it's built for service businesses that prioritize billing over deep bookkeeping.

faqs.q: Who should use FreshBooks instead of QuickBooks?
faqs.a: Solo operators and small service businesses (handymen, cleaners, painters, small crews) who want the easiest path to professional invoicing and getting paid, without accounting complexity. If you need deep accounting, inventory, job costing, many users, or the broadest FSM integration, QuickBooks or Xero is the better fit.

faqs.q: What are FreshBooks' client limits?
faqs.a: Lower tiers cap billable clients: Lite supports up to 5 and Plus up to 50. If you bill more clients than your tier allows, you'll need to move up a plan. For a small shop with a defined client base it's fine, but high-client-count businesses should plan for a higher tier or a different tool.

faqs.q: Does FreshBooks integrate with my field-service software?
faqs.a: Some FSM tools integrate with FreshBooks, but field-service platforms generally integrate with QuickBooks (and increasingly Xero) more deeply. If clean FSM-to-accounting sync is important to you, confirm your specific software supports FreshBooks before committing, since that integration is less universal.

software.name: Wave
software.tagline: Free accounting and invoicing for very small and new businesses
software.pricing_summary: Starter plan free ($0/mo) for core accounting and unlimited invoicing; Pro $16/mo adds automated bank imports, more users, and extras. Payments and Payroll are paid add-ons (Payroll from $40/mo + $6/employee)
software.long_description:
Wave is the best-known free accounting software for very small businesses — genuinely free core accounting and unlimited invoicing, with no monthly fee. Founded in 2009 in Toronto (and owned by H&R Block since 2019), it serves 2M+ small-business users who want to track income and expenses, send professional invoices, and keep basic books without paying a subscription. It earns solid ratings (4.4/5 on both G2 and Capterra; 4.6/5 on Software Advice across 1,600+ reviews).

For a brand-new or very small trades operator — a solo handyman, a side-hustle cleaner, a one-truck painter watching every dollar — Wave removes software cost as a barrier to getting organized. The free Starter plan covers core accounting and unlimited invoicing; a Pro plan ($16/mo) adds automated bank-transaction imports, more users, and extras; and Wave makes its money on add-ons — payment processing and Payroll (from $40/mo + $6/employee).

The trade-offs are depth, support, and the shrinking free tier. Wave is lighter than QuickBooks, Xero, or even FreshBooks on advanced accounting, reporting, and integrations, and the most common complaints are about customer support and the fact that some previously-free features have moved to the paid Pro plan over time. There are also fewer native FSM integrations. But for the specific job of free, simple accounting and invoicing for a very small business, Wave is the standout — just expect to graduate to a paid tool as you grow.
software.how_it_works: Wave is cloud accounting with a free core: you track income and expenses, send unlimited professional invoices, and keep basic double-entry books at no monthly cost on the Starter plan. The Pro plan ($16/mo) adds automated bank-feed imports, additional users, and extras. Wave monetizes through add-ons — accepting card/ACH payments on invoices (per-transaction fees) and Wave Payroll (from $40/mo plus $6 per employee/contractor) — so you can pay for only what you need. You connect a bank account (manually on free, automatically on Pro), categorize transactions, and run basic reports. There's no trial because the core plan is free to start.

pros_detail.title: Genuinely free core accounting
pros_detail.explanation: Wave's Starter plan offers real accounting and unlimited invoicing at $0/mo — removing software cost as a barrier for brand-new or very small trades businesses. Few competitors offer this.

pros_detail.title: Unlimited invoicing at no cost
pros_detail.explanation: Send unlimited professional invoices for free, which covers the single most important need for a small service business getting paid — without a subscription.

pros_detail.title: Solid ratings for a free tool
pros_detail.explanation: 4.4/5 on G2 and Capterra and 4.6/5 on Software Advice (1,600+ reviews) show Wave delivers real value, not just a stripped-down freebie, for its small-business target.

pros_detail.title: Pay only for add-ons you need
pros_detail.explanation: Payments and Payroll are optional paid add-ons, so you can run free accounting and only pay when you need to accept cards or run payroll — flexible for a lean operation.

pros_detail.title: Easy for non-accountants
pros_detail.explanation: Wave is simple and approachable, so a solo operator can keep basic books and invoice without accounting knowledge — ideal for getting organized early.

cons_detail.title: Lighter than paid competitors
cons_detail.explanation: Wave's accounting depth, reporting, and capabilities are lighter than QuickBooks, Xero, or FreshBooks. Growing trades shops that need inventory, job costing, or advanced reports will outgrow it.

cons_detail.title: Customer support complaints
cons_detail.explanation: The most common gripe is customer support — limited and slow, especially on the free plan. For a free tool that's expected, but it matters when you hit a problem with your books.

cons_detail.title: Free features shrinking over time
cons_detail.explanation: Some previously-free features have moved to the paid Pro plan, so the 'free' product is narrower than it once was. Confirm what's actually free today before relying on it.

cons_detail.title: Few native FSM integrations
cons_detail.explanation: Field-service tools rarely integrate with Wave the way they do with QuickBooks. If you want your FSM to sync to your books, Wave is usually not the tool.

cons_detail.title: You'll likely outgrow it
cons_detail.explanation: Wave is a great starting point, but most trades businesses graduate to QuickBooks, Xero, or FreshBooks as they add complexity. Plan for a migration down the road rather than assuming it scales.

software.best_for: Brand-new, solo, or very small trades businesses that want free, simple accounting and unlimited invoicing while watching every dollar
software.weaknesses: Lighter accounting/reporting than paid competitors; customer-support complaints; some previously-free features moved to paid Pro; few native FSM integrations; most shops outgrow it

ratings.reddit_sentiment: the go-to free option for tiny businesses; complaints about support and features moving from free to paid

software.affiliate_payout_note: No confirmed standard affiliate program (Wave is owned by H&R Block); affiliate_url stays TBD

faqs.q: Is Wave accounting still free in 2026?
faqs.a: Yes — Wave's Starter plan offers free core accounting and unlimited invoicing at $0/mo. However, some features that used to be free have moved to the paid Pro plan ($16/mo, adds automated bank imports and more), and Payments and Payroll are paid add-ons. So the core is free, but confirm which specific features you need are still on the free tier.

faqs.q: Who should use Wave?
faqs.a: Brand-new, solo, or very small trades businesses watching every dollar — a solo handyman, a side-hustle cleaner, a one-truck operator — who want free, simple accounting and unlimited invoicing. As you add employees, inventory, job costing, or need FSM integration, you'll likely graduate to QuickBooks, Xero, or FreshBooks.

faqs.q: What does Wave charge for?
faqs.a: Wave makes money on add-ons: the Pro plan ($16/mo) for automated bank imports and more users, payment processing (per-transaction fees to accept cards/ACH on invoices), and Wave Payroll (from $40/mo plus $6 per employee/contractor). The core accounting and invoicing remain free, so you only pay for the extras you use.

faqs.q: What are the downsides of Wave?
faqs.a: Mainly depth and support. Wave is lighter than paid competitors on advanced accounting, reporting, and integrations; customer support is limited (especially on the free plan); and some previously-free features have moved to paid. It also has few native field-service integrations, so it's not ideal if you need your FSM to sync to your books.

faqs.q: Will my field-service software sync with Wave?
faqs.a: Usually not natively. Field-service platforms overwhelmingly integrate with QuickBooks (and increasingly Xero), not Wave. If clean FSM-to-accounting sync matters to you, Wave is generally the wrong choice — it's best for very small businesses that manage invoicing and books directly in Wave rather than syncing from an FSM.

software.name: QuickBooks Desktop / Enterprise
software.tagline: The deepest job costing in the QuickBooks line — where established contractors graduate
software.pricing_summary: Annual subscription (reseller-sourced; Intuit pages bot-blocked): Silver ~$1,700–1,870/yr, Gold ~$2,210/yr (adds payroll + Contractor edition), Platinum ~$2,720/yr (Advanced Inventory/Pricing), Diamond ~$5,300+/yr (up to 40 users). Prices rose Feb 1, 2026. Confirm live before buying.
software.long_description:
QuickBooks Desktop Enterprise is the heavy-duty, Windows-installed end of the QuickBooks line, and its Contractor edition has the deepest job costing of any QuickBooks product — automatic cost-to-job tracking, change-order management, billing-rate levels (different labor rates for foreman vs. crew that auto-fill on invoices), and estimate-vs-actual reporting. It's where established US contractors graduate once QuickBooks Online's job costing stops being enough.

The big caveat is product direction: Intuit stopped selling new Pro/Premier/Mac Plus Desktop subscriptions to US customers after September 30, 2024, so Enterprise is effectively the only Desktop version you can still buy new — and it's expensive and rising (price increases landed February 1, 2026). It's Windows-only with no native field app, so contractors bolt on an FSM (Knowify and similar) for scheduling and dispatch. For a multi-crew shop running many simultaneous jobs, subs, and change orders, the job-costing depth justifies it; for a 1–3 person shop, it's overkill — QuickBooks Online, Zoho Books, or Striven fit better.
software.how_it_works: Enterprise installs locally or on a hosted server and supports up to 30–40 users sharing a company file. Bank and card feeds import transactions; you assign costs to jobs, build estimates, generate customer-facing change orders, set billing-rate levels by role, and run construction-specific reports (job profitability, estimates vs. actuals, committed costs). New Enterprise purchases typically run through Intuit's QuickBooks Solution Provider reseller channel rather than self-serve signup, with a 60-day money-back window.

pros_detail.title: Best-in-class job costing
pros_detail.explanation: Automatic cost-to-job allocation plus job-profitability and estimate-vs-actual reports — the deepest costing of any QuickBooks product, which is exactly what multi-job contractors need.

pros_detail.title: Built-in change orders
pros_detail.explanation: Track changes against the original estimate and auto-generate the customer change order — a construction workflow QuickBooks Online doesn't handle natively.

pros_detail.title: Billing-rate levels by role
pros_detail.explanation: Set different labor rates (foreman vs. apprentice) that auto-fill on invoices, so mixed-crew billing is accurate without manual edits.

pros_detail.title: Scales to 40 users + advanced inventory
pros_detail.explanation: Handles larger offices and shops carrying materials, with Advanced Inventory/Pricing on higher tiers — room established trades won't outgrow.

pros_detail.title: Mature accountant ecosystem
pros_detail.explanation: Every ProAdvisor and bookkeeper already knows QuickBooks, so finding fluent help and handing off at tax time is easy.

cons_detail.title: Desktop is being sunset
cons_detail.explanation: Intuit stopped selling new Pro/Premier/Mac Plus Desktop subscriptions after Sept 30, 2024; Enterprise is the only Desktop version still buyable new — a real long-term-direction risk to weigh.

cons_detail.title: Expensive and rising
cons_detail.explanation: Annual cost runs from ~$1,700 to $5,300+, with price increases on Feb 1, 2026. The tier you actually need can cost far more than a cloud rival.

cons_detail.title: Windows-only, no native field app
cons_detail.explanation: There's no first-party mobile/field tool — contractors pair it with a separate FSM for scheduling and dispatch.

cons_detail.title: Heavy support / Payments-hold complaints
cons_detail.explanation: Trustpilot skews very negative on support waits and QuickBooks Payments fund holds — budget for self-reliance or a ProAdvisor.

cons_detail.title: Overkill for small shops
cons_detail.explanation: For a solo or 1–3-person operation, the cost and complexity outweigh the job-costing depth — lighter tools fit better.

software.best_for: Established GCs and specialty trades (HVAC, electrical, plumbing) running multiple simultaneous jobs, subcontractors, and change orders, who need the deepest QuickBooks job costing and can absorb the cost
software.weaknesses: Desktop sunset (Enterprise-only for new buyers); expensive and rising (Feb 2026 hike); Windows-only with no native field app; heavy support/Payments-hold complaints; overkill for solo/small shops

ratings.reddit_sentiment: regarded as the deepest job costing in the QuickBooks line and the place established contractors graduate to — but criticized as Desktop-only, pricey, and subject to rising annual fees

software.affiliate_program: QuickBooks affiliate program exists (skews to QBO); new Enterprise deals typically flow through the QuickBooks Solution Provider reseller channel, not self-serve affiliate links
software.affiliate_payout_note: affiliate_url stays TBD — Enterprise is reseller-channel (QSP), not a simple affiliate link

faqs.q: Is QuickBooks Desktop being discontinued?
faqs.a: Intuit stopped selling new Pro, Premier, and Mac Plus Desktop subscriptions to US customers after September 30, 2024. QuickBooks Enterprise is the only Desktop version you can still buy new in 2026. Existing Pro/Premier subscribers can renew, but new buyers who want Desktop must go to Enterprise — factor that long-term direction into the decision.

faqs.q: Why do contractors use QuickBooks Enterprise over QuickBooks Online?
faqs.a: Job costing depth. Enterprise's Contractor edition adds automatic cost-to-job tracking, change orders, billing-rate levels, and estimate-vs-actual reporting that QuickBooks Online can't match. Multi-crew contractors running many simultaneous jobs graduate to Enterprise for that; smaller shops are usually fine on QBO.

faqs.q: How much does QuickBooks Enterprise cost in 2026?
faqs.a: Roughly $1,700–$1,870/yr (Silver), ~$2,210/yr (Gold, adds payroll + Contractor edition), ~$2,720/yr (Platinum, Advanced Inventory/Pricing), and $5,300+/yr (Diamond, up to 40 users). Intuit raised prices on Feb 1, 2026. Confirm current pricing with a reseller before buying.

faqs.q: Does QuickBooks Enterprise have a field/mobile app for techs?
faqs.a: No native one. Enterprise is Windows-installed accounting; contractors pair it with a field-service tool (Knowify is a common companion for construction) for scheduling, dispatch, and mobile job tracking, then sync to Enterprise for the books.

software.name: Sage (Sage 50 + Sage Accounting)
software.tagline: Real job costing and inventory in a desktop-grade package — plus a cheap cloud tier
software.pricing_summary: Sage 50 desktop (with job costing): Pro ~$124/mo (1 user), Premium ~$169/user/mo, Quantum ~$253/user/mo (up to ~40 users); 1-year commitment. Sage Accounting cloud: Start ~$10/mo, Standard ~$25/mo (often 70% off first 6 months). Sage US pages block automated fetch — confirm live.
software.headquartered: Newcastle upon Tyne, UK (US ops in Georgia)
software.long_description:
Sage is one of the oldest names in small-business accounting, and for trades its draw is genuine job costing and inventory in Sage 50 — the desktop-grade product (descended from Peachtree) that supports jobs broken into phases and cost codes for budget-vs-actual tracking, plus progress/AIA-style billing. That's a level of construction costing most cloud-native tools don't reach. Alongside it, Sage Accounting is a lighter browser/mobile cloud product starting around $10/mo for solos who just need invoicing and bank reconciliation.

The trade-offs are price and product confusion. Sage 50 is pricey next to cloud rivals (from ~$124/mo) and is desktop-first with weaker Mac/mobile; the two product lines (Sage 50 vs. Sage Accounting) confuse buyers; and Sage draws recurring complaints about price increases, billing, and slow support. But if you need real job costing and inventory without jumping to a full ERP, Sage 50 is a credible middle ground, with a clear upgrade path into Sage's dedicated construction/specialty-contractor suite as you grow.
software.how_it_works: Sage Accounting runs in the browser and mobile app; Sage 50 is a Windows application with cloud connectivity. Bank feeds auto-match transactions; Sage 50 adds jobs → phases → cost codes so you can budget a project and track actuals against it, run progress billing, and manage inventory and purchase orders. Sage offers a free guided migration from QuickBooks (a one-way data import, not a live sync). Pricing is subscription, with Sage 50 typically on an annual commitment.

pros_detail.title: Native job costing with phases and cost codes
pros_detail.explanation: Sage 50 tracks jobs at the phase/cost-code level for budget-vs-actual — real construction costing without moving to a full ERP.

pros_detail.title: Inventory and progress billing
pros_detail.explanation: Handles materials inventory, purchase orders, and progress/AIA-style billing that trades doing project work actually need.

pros_detail.title: Free QuickBooks migration
pros_detail.explanation: Sage offers a guided one-way import from QuickBooks, lowering the switching cost if you're outgrowing QBO's costing.

pros_detail.title: Clear upgrade path to construction suite
pros_detail.explanation: If you scale, Sage's dedicated Construction and specialty-contractor products are a natural next step within the same vendor.

pros_detail.title: Cheap cloud entry for solos
pros_detail.explanation: Sage Accounting from ~$10/mo gives a low-cost on-ramp for one-person shops that don't need Sage 50's depth yet.

cons_detail.title: Pricey desktop product
cons_detail.explanation: Sage 50 starts around $124/mo (1 user) and rises per user — meaningfully more than Xero, Zoho Books, or QuickBooks Online at comparable tiers.

cons_detail.title: Two confusing product lines
cons_detail.explanation: Sage 50 (desktop, job costing) vs. Sage Accounting (cloud, lighter) trip up buyers — it's easy to subscribe to the wrong one.

cons_detail.title: Billing and price-increase complaints
cons_detail.explanation: Trustpilot shows a recurring pattern of subscription price hikes, billing/refund friction, and slow support.

cons_detail.title: Desktop-first, weak Mac/mobile
cons_detail.explanation: Sage 50's strength is on Windows; Mac and mobile experiences lag the cloud-native competition.

cons_detail.title: No native FSM scheduling/dispatch
cons_detail.explanation: It's accounting, not field service — you still pair it with an FSM (or move up to Sage's construction suite) for dispatch.

software.best_for: Established contractors who need real job costing, phases/cost codes, and inventory without jumping to a full ERP — with solos able to start on the ~$10/mo cloud tier
software.weaknesses: Sage 50 is pricey vs cloud rivals; two confusing product lines; recurring billing/price-increase and slow-support complaints; desktop-first with weak Mac/mobile; no native field service

ratings.reddit_sentiment: respected for real job costing and inventory in Sage 50, but buyers are confused by the two product lines and gripe about price increases and support

software.affiliate_program: Yes — Sage Affiliate Program (via Impact.com) plus the Sage Partner Network
software.affiliate_payout_note: affiliate_url stays TBD until the Impact.com tracking link is confirmed

faqs.q: Does Sage do job costing for contractors?
faqs.a: Yes — Sage 50 (the desktop product) supports job costing with phases and cost codes for budget-vs-actual tracking, plus inventory and progress billing. The lighter Sage Accounting cloud product does not have that depth, so for construction costing you specifically want Sage 50 (or Sage's dedicated construction suite as you scale).

faqs.q: Sage 50 vs Sage Accounting — which do trades need?
faqs.a: Sage 50 if you need job costing, cost codes, inventory, and progress billing (most established trades doing project work). Sage Accounting (~$10/mo) if you're a solo who just needs invoicing, expenses, and bank reconciliation. The two are separate products — pick by whether job costing matters to you.

faqs.q: How much does Sage cost for a small contractor?
faqs.a: Sage Accounting cloud starts around $10/mo (Start) / $25/mo (Standard), often discounted ~70% for the first six months. Sage 50 desktop with job costing starts around $124/mo for one user and scales per user. Sage's US pages block automated checking, so confirm current pricing on their site.

faqs.q: Can I move from QuickBooks to Sage?
faqs.a: Yes — Sage offers a free guided migration that imports your QuickBooks data. Note it's a one-way data import to get you set up, not an ongoing live sync between the two. It lowers the switching cost if you're moving to Sage 50 for deeper job costing.

software.name: Zoho Books
software.tagline: The best-value full accounting — a real free tier and built-in project tracking
software.pricing_summary: Free $0/mo (revenue under $50K/yr) · Standard ~$15/mo · Professional ~$40/mo · Premium ~$60/mo · Elite ~$120/mo · Ultimate ~$240/mo (annual-effective). Extra users +$3/mo. 14-day trial on paid tiers.
software.long_description:
Zoho Books is the strongest value play in trades accounting: a genuine free tier for businesses under $50K/yr in revenue, then full double-entry accounting from about $15/mo — invoicing, estimates, bills and POs, expense and mileage tracking, bank reconciliation, inventory, and (unusually at this price) built-in project time-tracking so you can see per-job margins. It markets itself squarely as a QuickBooks alternative, and for cost-conscious solo and small trades it delivers most of what they need for a fraction of QBO's cost.

The catches are ecosystem-shaped. Zoho's US payroll only fully manages around 21 states, so multi-state crews need third-party payroll; support responsiveness is the most common complaint; field scheduling lives in a separate product (Zoho FSM), not inside Books; and it isn't construction-specific (no deep AIA/retainage job costing). But if you want affordable, capable full accounting — especially if you'd use other Zoho apps (CRM, Inventory, FSM) — Zoho Books is hard to beat on value.
software.how_it_works: Connect bank and card feeds (or import), send branded invoices and estimates, accept online payments through a client portal, and let Zoho auto-categorize and reconcile. Projects and time-tracking attach costs and hours to jobs for margin reporting. Books runs natively inside the wider Zoho suite, so data flows to Zoho CRM, Inventory, Projects, Payroll, and the separate Zoho FSM field-service product; 1,000+ further apps connect via Zoho Flow or Zapier.

pros_detail.title: Real free tier, cheap paid plans
pros_detail.explanation: Free under $50K/yr revenue and ~$15/mo Standard — the lowest genuine cost of any full-accounting tool here, ideal for solo trades.

pros_detail.title: Built-in project + time tracking
pros_detail.explanation: Attach time and costs to jobs to see per-project margins — a job-costing-lite capability rare at this price point.

pros_detail.title: Strong invoicing and client portal
pros_detail.explanation: Branded invoices, estimates, online payments, and a customer portal make getting paid smooth for field businesses.

pros_detail.title: Same-vendor field service (Zoho FSM)
pros_detail.explanation: If you need scheduling/dispatch, Zoho's own FSM product pushes jobs and invoices into Books — one ecosystem, one login.

pros_detail.title: Sales tax and 1099/W-9 handling
pros_detail.explanation: Handles US sales tax and contractor 1099/W-9 workflows, covering the tax-time basics trades need.

cons_detail.title: US payroll covers ~21 states only
cons_detail.explanation: Zoho Payroll fully manages around 21 states; multi-state crews need a third-party payroll provider alongside Books.

cons_detail.title: Support responsiveness
cons_detail.explanation: The most common complaint is slow or hard-to-reach support — a real consideration when something breaks at month-end.

cons_detail.title: Field service is a separate product
cons_detail.explanation: Scheduling/dispatch isn't inside Books — you add Zoho FSM, so it's not a single all-in-one out of the box.

cons_detail.title: Best value only inside the Zoho world
cons_detail.explanation: The economics shine when you use other Zoho apps; as a standalone it's still good but you leave value on the table.

cons_detail.title: Not construction-specific
cons_detail.explanation: No deep AIA billing, retainage, or heavy job costing — fine for most trades, light for complex GC accounting.

software.best_for: Cost-conscious solo and small trades (plumbers, electricians, HVAC, handymen, small GCs) wanting affordable full accounting with light per-job tracking — especially if using other Zoho apps
software.weaknesses: US payroll fully covers only ~21 states; support responsiveness is the common gripe; field service is a separate product (Zoho FSM); best value only inside the Zoho ecosystem; not construction-specific

ratings.reddit_sentiment: widely praised as the best-value full accounting, especially inside the Zoho suite; the recurring knock is support responsiveness

software.affiliate_program: Yes — Zoho Affiliate Program: 15% commission for the first 12 months (up to ~18–20% at higher tiers), 90-day cookie
software.affiliate_payout_note: affiliate_url stays TBD until the Zoho affiliate tracking link is confirmed

faqs.q: Is Zoho Books really free for a small contractor?
faqs.a: Yes — Zoho Books has a genuine free plan for businesses with under $50,000/yr in revenue, covering invoicing, expenses, and reconciliation for one user. Above that you move to Standard (~$15/mo). It's the cheapest legitimate full-accounting on-ramp for a solo trade.

faqs.q: Does Zoho Books do job costing?
faqs.a: It has light job costing via its Projects and time-tracking features — you can attach time and costs to a job and see per-project margins. It does not do construction-grade AIA billing or retainage, so for complex GC accounting it's lighter than Sage 50 or QuickBooks Enterprise, but it's plenty for most service trades.

faqs.q: Can Zoho Books handle my payroll?
faqs.a: Zoho Payroll fully supports around 21 US states. If your crew is in a covered state it integrates cleanly; if you operate across multiple states (including uncovered ones), you'll pair Books with a third-party payroll provider like Gusto or OnPay.

faqs.q: Does Zoho Books work for field-service scheduling?
faqs.a: Not by itself — scheduling and dispatch live in a separate product, Zoho FSM, which pushes jobs and invoices into Books. If you want one vendor for both accounting and field service, the Zoho suite covers it, but Books alone is accounting-only.

software.name: Patriot Accounting
software.tagline: Dirt-cheap, dead-simple US bookkeeping — best paired with Patriot Payroll
software.pricing_summary: Accounting Basic ~$20/mo (promo ~$10/mo first 6 months) · Accounting Premium ~$30/mo (promo ~$15/mo first 6 months). Monthly billing, US-only. 30-day free trial.
software.long_description:
Patriot Accounting is the budget, no-frills pick: cheap (~$20–30/mo, frequently half-off for the first six months), genuinely easy for a non-accountant owner, and US-focused. You get unlimited invoices, customers, and vendors, automatic bank imports with AI categorization via Plaid, online card/ACH payments through Stripe, financial reports, and a patented dual-ledger (cash/accrual) toggle. Its real superpower is pairing with Patriot Payroll — books and paychecks from one inexpensive vendor, which is a clean, cheap stack for a small US trade.

The limits are the flip side of the simplicity: it's US-only, there's no native mobile app (mobile web only), and job costing is limited-to-absent, which is a real gap for GCs and HVAC shops that live on per-job profitability. It also doesn't act as a QuickBooks sync — it's a replacement, not a companion. But for a cost-conscious solo plumber, electrician, or handyman who wants tidy books and cheap payroll without QuickBooks' price or complexity, Patriot is excellent value and unusually well-reviewed.
software.how_it_works: At setup you import your existing chart of accounts. Bank and card transactions auto-import via Plaid (roughly two years of history) with AI categorization for fast reconciliation. You send unlimited invoices and take online payments via Stripe (cards, Apple/Google Pay, ACH). Premium adds estimates, recurring invoices, payment reminders, user permissions, receipt management, and subaccounts. If you add Patriot Payroll, the two share data so books and paychecks stay in sync.

pros_detail.title: Very low price
pros_detail.explanation: About $20–30/mo with frequent 50%-off-first-6-months promos — among the cheapest real accounting tools for a US trade.

pros_detail.title: Unlimited invoices, customers, vendors
pros_detail.explanation: No caps on the core records, so a growing customer list never pushes you up a pricing tier.

pros_detail.title: Tight Patriot Payroll integration
pros_detail.explanation: Run books and payroll from one low-cost vendor with shared data — a clean, cheap stack for small shops.

pros_detail.title: Automatic bank imports + reconciliation
pros_detail.explanation: Plaid-powered feeds with AI categorization keep reconciliation fast without manual entry.

pros_detail.title: Free contractor/1099 portal + dual-ledger
pros_detail.explanation: Includes a 1099 contractor portal and a patented cash/accrual toggle — handy at tax time for trades using subs.

cons_detail.title: US-only
cons_detail.explanation: Built for US tax and banking; not an option for the UK/Canada/other markets in this directory.

cons_detail.title: No native mobile app
cons_detail.explanation: It's mobile-web only — fine for office work, but there's no dedicated app for invoicing from the field.

cons_detail.title: Limited/no job costing
cons_detail.explanation: Per-job profitability is weak to absent — a real gap for GCs and HVAC shops that need true job costing.

cons_detail.title: Doesn't sync with QuickBooks
cons_detail.explanation: Patriot is a QuickBooks replacement, not a companion — there's no live two-way sync (payroll can export IIF only).

cons_detail.title: Direct-deposit / check-printing friction
cons_detail.explanation: The payroll side draws occasional complaints about strict/slow direct deposit and check-print alignment.

software.best_for: Cost-conscious solo and micro US trades (plumber, electrician, small HVAC, handyman) wanting tidy, simple books — ideally bundled with cheap Patriot Payroll
software.weaknesses: US-only; no native mobile app (mobile web only); limited/no job costing; not a QuickBooks sync (it replaces it); occasional direct-deposit/check-printing friction on the payroll side

ratings.reddit_sentiment: consistently praised as cheap, simple, and well-supported for solo/micro businesses; the known limit is weak job costing

software.affiliate_program: Yes — Patriot Affiliate Program (~$150 per new paying customer) plus an Accountant Partner Program
software.affiliate_payout_note: affiliate_url stays TBD until the Patriot affiliate tracking link is confirmed

faqs.q: How much does Patriot Accounting cost?
faqs.a: Accounting Basic is about $20/mo and Premium about $30/mo, billed monthly, with frequent promos cutting the first six months to roughly $10–15/mo. There's a 30-day free trial. It's among the cheapest legitimate accounting options for a US trades business.

faqs.q: Is Patriot good for contractors with job costing needs?
faqs.a: Less so — Patriot's strengths are price and simplicity, and its job costing is limited to absent. If per-job profitability is central to your business (most GCs, HVAC, remodelers), Sage 50, QuickBooks Enterprise, or Striven fit better. Patriot shines for solo/micro trades that just need clean books.

faqs.q: Does Patriot Accounting work with Patriot Payroll?
faqs.a: Yes, and that's its best use case. The accounting and payroll products share data, so running both gives a small US trade tidy books plus compliant paychecks from one inexpensive vendor — a notably cheaper stack than QuickBooks plus a separate payroll service.

faqs.q: Can Patriot sync with QuickBooks?
faqs.a: No — Patriot is designed to replace QuickBooks, not run alongside it. There's no live two-way sync (the payroll side can export an IIF file). If you want to keep QuickBooks as your books, Patriot isn't the companion tool; if you want to leave QuickBooks for something cheaper, it's a strong target.

software.name: Striven
software.tagline: All-in-one ERP-lite built for contractors — native accounting + dispatch in one system
software.pricing_summary: Standard ~$35/user/mo · Enterprise ~$70/user/mo · +$25/user surcharge under 5 users. Customer portals free to 100 users, ~$99/mo to 500, ~$499/mo unlimited. Free "test drive" available (length unverified).
software.long_description:
Striven is the rare tool that's genuinely built for trades as an all-in-one: native double-entry accounting plus CRM, project and task management, inventory, HR, and — crucially — scheduling and dispatch, all in one system with dedicated HVAC, plumbing, electrical, and construction solution pages. Instead of syncing an FSM to QuickBooks, Striven replaces both: estimates → jobs → field labor/material costing → invoicing → financials flow through a single platform, so job-costing data isn't stranded in a separate tool.

It's not for everyone. There's a real learning curve and longer onboarding (the most-cited drawback), built-in payroll is limited (you add an integration), and per-user pricing plus portal fees add up at scale — it's over-built for a 1–2 person shop. But Striven has an outstanding support reputation, construction is its single largest reviewer segment, and for a growing crew that wants to ditch the QuickBooks-plus-spreadsheets-plus-scheduling-app patchwork for one system, it's one of the few products here actually designed for that.
software.how_it_works: Striven unifies operations and accounting in one cloud platform: field data (job costing, labor, materials, equipment) flows directly into native accounting, so contractors run the whole estimate-to-financials cycle in one place. You manage CRM and sales, schedule and dispatch crews, track projects on Kanban/Gantt views, handle inventory and procurement, and give customers and vendors portal access. Migrating from QuickBooks imports your data into Striven's own accounting (it replaces QuickBooks rather than syncing to it). Pricing is per user, with separate tiers for customer-portal volume.

pros_detail.title: Genuine built-in accounting
pros_detail.explanation: Striven has native double-entry accounting (GL, AP, AR, financials) — it replaces QuickBooks rather than syncing to it, so your books and operations share one database.

pros_detail.title: Real field-service depth
pros_detail.explanation: Scheduling/dispatch, job costing, subcontractor credential tracking, inventory, and fleet are built in — not bolted on via a third-party FSM.

pros_detail.title: Industry-specific solutions
pros_detail.explanation: Dedicated HVAC, plumbing, electrical, and construction configurations mean it speaks trades out of the box, not generic SMB.

pros_detail.title: Unlimited users on every plan
pros_detail.explanation: Plans are per-user but not capped, so the whole crew and office can be in the system without hitting seat limits.

pros_detail.title: Outstanding support reputation
pros_detail.explanation: Striven wins high marks for customer support (and awards for it) — a real differentiator for a system this broad.

cons_detail.title: Learning curve / longer onboarding
cons_detail.explanation: The most-cited drawback: a broad all-in-one takes time to set up and adopt versus a single-purpose app.

cons_detail.title: Limited built-in payroll
cons_detail.explanation: Payroll isn't fully native — you add an integration or service, so it's not quite everything-in-one for paychecks.

cons_detail.title: Search and reporting gaps
cons_detail.explanation: Reviewers note search and some reporting could be stronger relative to the platform's breadth.

cons_detail.title: Per-user cost adds up
cons_detail.explanation: $35–70/user plus portal fees at scale can get expensive for a larger team versus a flat-rate tool.

cons_detail.title: Over-built for a 1–2 person shop
cons_detail.explanation: A solo operator pays for (and must learn) far more platform than they need — lighter tools fit better at that size.

software.best_for: Small-to-mid contractors (HVAC, plumbing, electrical, GC) who want to replace the QuickBooks-plus-scheduling-plus-CRM patchwork with one platform that has native accounting and dispatch
software.weaknesses: Learning curve and longer onboarding; limited built-in payroll (needs integration); search/reporting gaps; per-user + portal cost adds up at scale; over-built for a 1–2 person shop

ratings.reddit_sentiment: regarded as a genuinely contractor-focused all-in-one with excellent support; the consistent caveat is the onboarding learning curve

software.affiliate_program: Yes — a Partner/VAR program (recurring commission, the stronger path) plus a true affiliate program via Impact.com (low rate)
software.affiliate_payout_note: affiliate_url stays TBD; the VAR/partner track is the more meaningful monetization route than the affiliate link

faqs.q: Is Striven actually built for contractors?
faqs.a: Yes — Striven has dedicated HVAC, plumbing, electrical, and construction solutions, and construction is its single largest reviewer segment. It combines native accounting with scheduling/dispatch, job costing, inventory, and subcontractor tracking, so it's designed for trades rather than being a generic SMB tool.

faqs.q: Does Striven replace QuickBooks?
faqs.a: Yes — Striven has its own native double-entry accounting and is meant to replace QuickBooks, not sync to it. Migrating imports your QuickBooks data into Striven. That's the appeal for contractors tired of running QuickBooks plus a separate scheduling app plus spreadsheets — it consolidates them.

faqs.q: How much does Striven cost?
faqs.a: About $35/user/mo (Standard) or $70/user/mo (Enterprise), with a $25/user surcharge if you have fewer than 5 users, plus customer-portal tiers (free to 100 users, ~$99/mo to 500, ~$499/mo unlimited). It's per-user, so model your headcount; it's most cost-effective for a real crew, not a solo operator.

faqs.q: What is the main downside of Striven?
faqs.a: The learning curve. Because it's a broad all-in-one (accounting + CRM + projects + dispatch + inventory + HR), onboarding takes longer than a single-purpose app, and it's over-built for a 1–2 person shop. Its support reputation is strong, which helps, but plan for a real setup period.

software.name: Melio
software.tagline: Free ACH bill-pay that syncs to your books — pay subs and suppliers, even check-only ones
software.pricing_summary: Go $0/mo (5 free ACH/mo) · Core ~$25/mo (+$10/user) · Boost ~$55/mo · Unlimited ~$80/mo (unlimited free ACH; required for QuickBooks Desktop + NetSuite sync). Fees: ACH $0.50, card 2.9%, mailed check $1.50, instant transfer 1%.
software.headquartered: New York, New York (a Xero company since Oct 2025)
software.long_description:
Melio isn't full accounting — it's accounts-payable / bill-pay that sits alongside your books and pays your vendors, subs, and suppliers. Its appeal for trades: free ACH bill payments on the entry plan, the ability to pay even check-only vendors (Melio mails the check), and the option to float a job by paying a supplier with a card (2.9%) even where cards aren't accepted — then it two-way syncs to QuickBooks or Xero so everything reconciles. Melio was acquired by Xero (~$2.5B, completed October 2025), so it's now a well-backed part of a major accounting vendor.

The honest caveat is reliability of access: Melio has a well-documented pattern of sudden account freezes and held payments during opaque compliance reviews, with weak support for suspended accounts, and BBB rates it F. It's a legitimate, large-scale, Xero-owned service — but because of the freeze risk, don't make it the single point of failure for a time-critical vendor payment without a backup. Used as intended (free ACH bill-pay that reconciles to your books), it's a genuinely useful, low-cost addition to a trades stack.
software.how_it_works: Enter or import a vendor bill, choose how to pay (free ACH, card to defer cash, or have Melio mail a paper check), and Melio delivers the payment by the vendor's preferred method — the vendor doesn't need a Melio account. Approval workflows let an owner sign off on payments an office manager queues. Everything two-way syncs to QuickBooks Online (Desktop and NetSuite on the Unlimited plan) and Xero so your books stay reconciled. It's outgoing AP plus payment-request links for AR — not a general ledger.

pros_detail.title: Free ACH bill pay
pros_detail.explanation: Pay subcontractors and suppliers by ACH at $0 on the entry plan — a genuine cash cost saved versus card or wire fees.

pros_detail.title: Pays check-only vendors
pros_detail.explanation: For suppliers that won't take electronic payment, Melio mails a paper check for you, so you manage everything from one place.

pros_detail.title: Float a job on a card
pros_detail.explanation: Pay a supplier with a credit card (2.9%) even where cards aren't accepted, deferring cash outlay until the customer pays you.

pros_detail.title: Two-way QuickBooks/Xero sync
pros_detail.explanation: Payments reconcile automatically against your books, eliminating double-entry — and Melio is now Xero-owned.

pros_detail.title: Free base plan + approval workflows
pros_detail.explanation: A $0 entry tier plus owner/office-manager approval roles make it easy to adopt without new cost or losing control.

cons_detail.title: Account freezes / payment holds
cons_detail.explanation: A well-documented pattern of sudden freezes and held payments during compliance reviews — risky if it's your only path for a time-critical payment.

cons_detail.title: Weak support for suspended accounts
cons_detail.explanation: When an account is flagged, support is largely email-only and slow, which compounds the freeze problem.

cons_detail.title: Not full accounting
cons_detail.explanation: Melio is AP/bill-pay only — you still need QuickBooks, Xero, or similar as your actual books.

cons_detail.title: 2.9% card fee on big invoices
cons_detail.explanation: Floating a large material invoice on a card costs 2.9%, which adds up fast on big-ticket purchases.

cons_detail.title: Best sync gated behind Unlimited
cons_detail.explanation: QuickBooks Desktop and NetSuite sync require the ~$80/mo Unlimited plan, so the cheapest tiers miss those integrations.

software.best_for: Small US contractors (GC, electrical, plumbing, HVAC, landscaping) who pay subs and suppliers and want free ACH bill-pay that reconciles to QuickBooks or Xero — used with a backup for time-critical payments
software.weaknesses: Documented sudden account freezes / held payments during compliance reviews; weak support for suspended accounts (BBB rates it F); not full accounting; 2.9% card fee; best integrations gated behind the $80/mo plan
software.reputation_flag: Documented pattern of sudden account freezes and held payments during automated compliance reviews, with email-only support for suspended accounts; BBB rates Melio F. Legitimate and Xero-owned, but do not make it the single point of failure for a time-critical vendor payment.

ratings.reddit_sentiment: liked for free ACH bill-pay and QuickBooks/Xero sync, but with a recurring and serious complaint pattern around sudden account freezes and held payments

software.affiliate_program: Yes — Melio Affiliate Program via PartnerStack (reported ~$300 per referred business / ~$600 per referred accounting firm client, 90-day cookie)
software.affiliate_payout_note: affiliate_url stays TBD until the PartnerStack tracking link and exact payout are confirmed

faqs.q: Is Melio accounting software?
faqs.a: No — Melio is accounts-payable / bill-pay, not a general ledger. It pays your vendors and subs (by ACH, check, card, or wire) and syncs those payments to QuickBooks or Xero, but you still need a real accounting tool as your books. Think of it as the bill-pay layer on top of your accounting.

faqs.q: How does Melio make bill-pay free?
faqs.a: The entry plan includes free ACH (bank-to-bank) payments — you pay $0 to send a sub or supplier money by ACH. Melio earns on card payments (2.9%), instant transfers (1%), mailed checks ($1.50), and paid monthly plans. For a contractor paying vendors by ACH and reconciling to QuickBooks, the core use is genuinely free.

faqs.q: Is Melio safe to rely on?
faqs.a: It's a legitimate, large-scale service now owned by Xero — but there's a well-documented pattern of sudden account freezes and held payments during compliance reviews, with slow support for flagged accounts (BBB rates it F). Use it for routine ACH bill-pay, but keep a backup payment method for time-critical vendor payments rather than relying on it as a single point of failure.

faqs.q: Can Melio pay a supplier who only takes checks?
faqs.a: Yes — that's one of its best features. You schedule the payment in Melio and it mails a paper check to the vendor on your behalf ($1.50), even though the vendor isn't a Melio user. You can pay every vendor from one place regardless of how each one wants to be paid.
